stem:
A marine biologist studied kelp forest recovery after sea otter reintroduction at four sites along the Pacific coast. The table below shows kelp density (plants per square meter) measured in 2020 and 2024.

| Site | 2020 density | 2024 density |
|---|---|---|
| Cape Ridge | 2.1 | 9.4 |
| Stonepoint | 1.8 | 8.7 |
| Driftwood Bay | 2.4 | 10.2 |
| Harbor Cove | 1.9 | 9.1 |

The biologist claims that kelp density at every studied site more than tripled between 2020 and 2024. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Kelp density at Driftwood Bay reached 10.2 plants per square meter in 2024.
Harbor Cove had the lowest starting density at 1.9 plants per square meter.
At all four sites, 2024 kelp density was more than three times the 2020 value, with Cape Ridge climbing from 2.1 to 9.4 and Stonepoint from 1.8 to 8.7.
Kelp density increased at every site between 2020 and 2024.

explanation: The claim requires showing every site more than tripled. Only choice C cites specific values demonstrating that each ratio exceeds 3x; the other options either name only one site or merely note an increase without quantifying the ratio.

stem:
A school district tested three reading intervention programs with struggling third graders. The table shows the average number of reading levels students advanced over one semester.

| Program | Levels advanced |
|---|---|
| Standard tutoring | 0.8 |
| Phonics-focused | 1.6 |
| Peer reading | 1.1 |

The district superintendent claims that the phonics-focused program produced the greatest average gains among third graders. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Students in the standard tutoring program advanced 0.8 reading levels.
The peer reading program was moderately effective for third graders.
The phonics-focused program advanced students an average of 1.6 reading levels, higher than both peer reading (1.1) and standard tutoring (0.8).
All three programs showed some benefit for struggling readers.

explanation: Supporting a 'greatest gains' claim requires naming the top value and comparing it to the others. Only choice C does this with exact figures from the table, showing phonics (1.6) beats peer reading (1.1) and standard tutoring (0.8).

stem:
A community garden tracked tomato yields from four varieties grown under identical conditions. The table shows total harvest weight per plant.

| Variety | Yield (lbs/plant) |
|---|---|
| Brandywine | 6.2 |
| San Marzano | 8.4 |
| Cherokee Purple | 7.1 |
| Roma | 9.8 |

A garden coordinator writing a newsletter wants to recommend Roma as the highest-yielding variety in the trial. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this recommendation?

choices.text: Cherokee Purple produced 7.1 pounds per plant.
Brandywine had the lowest yield in the trial at 6.2 pounds.
Roma plants produced 9.8 pounds per plant, more than any other variety in the trial.
All four varieties produced more than 5 pounds per plant.

explanation: A 'highest-yielding' recommendation requires stating Roma's top value and ranking it against the others. Only choice C names Roma's 9.8 lbs/plant and makes that direct comparison to the rest of the trial.

stem:
A bike-share company compared monthly ridership in three neighborhoods after adding docking stations. The table shows rides per month in June 2025.

| Neighborhood | Rides (June 2025) |
|---|---|
| Greenvale | 3,200 |
| Riverside | 5,900 |
| Hilltop | 1,400 |

A company analyst claims that Riverside had substantially more bike-share use than the other neighborhoods in June. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Riverside recorded 5,900 rides in June, nearly double Greenvale's 3,200 and more than four times Hilltop's 1,400.
Hilltop had the fewest docking stations of the three neighborhoods.
Bike-share popularity varies widely by neighborhood.
Greenvale had 3,200 rides in June.

explanation: To show Riverside had 'substantially more' use, the answer must quantify the gap across all three neighborhoods. Only choice A does this, comparing 5,900 rides to Greenvale's 3,200 and Hilltop's 1,400.

stem:
Researchers recorded daily average screen time for teens who participated in either an outdoor-activity club or a control group. The table below summarizes one month of data.

| Group | Avg. screen time (hrs/day) |
|---|---|
| Outdoor club | 3.2 |
| Control | 5.7 |

A researcher claims that teens in the outdoor club spent noticeably less time on screens than those in the control group. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Outdoor-club teens are generally more active than their peers.
Control-group teens averaged 5.7 hours per day on screens.
Teens in the outdoor club averaged 3.2 hours per day on screens, compared with 5.7 hours per day for the control group — a difference of 2.5 hours per day.
Many teens report spending a lot of time on screens each day.

explanation: A 'noticeably less' claim requires both values and the size of the gap. Only choice C pulls both numbers from the table (3.2 vs. 5.7) and reports the 2.5-hour difference.

stem:
A nonprofit tracked how many meals it served at four shelters during the 2024 holiday season. The table shows total meals served per shelter.

| Shelter | Meals served |
|---|---|
| Eastside | 4,100 |
| Northpark | 6,500 |
| Westview | 2,800 |
| Southgate | 3,900 |

The nonprofit's director claims that Northpark served more holiday meals than any other shelter in 2024. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Northpark served 6,500 holiday meals, the highest total among the four shelters in the table.
Westview served the fewest holiday meals at 2,800.
Eastside and Southgate both served more than 3,500 meals.
All four shelters served thousands of meals during the holiday season.

explanation: The claim asks for a top-value comparison. Only choice A states Northpark's figure (6,500) and explicitly identifies it as the highest in the table.

stem:
A physics teacher measured the drop time of a ball from four different heights, averaging three trials at each height. The table summarizes the results.

| Height (m) | Avg. drop time (s) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.45 |
| 2 | 0.64 |
| 4 | 0.90 |
| 8 | 1.28 |

The teacher claims that drop time increases as the drop height increases. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Drop time rose steadily from 0.45 s at 1 m to 0.64 s at 2 m, 0.90 s at 4 m, and 1.28 s at 8 m.
The 4-meter drop took 0.90 seconds on average.
The 1-meter drop had the shortest average drop time.
Ball drops are a common physics demonstration.

explanation: Supporting an 'increases as height increases' claim requires showing the trend across all heights. Only choice A lists every height/time pair to demonstrate the monotonic rise from 0.45 s to 1.28 s.

stem:
Economists studying remote work surveyed employees at three types of companies about their weekly commute hours before and after a shift to hybrid schedules. The table shows average commute hours per week.

| Company type | Pre-hybrid (hrs/wk) | Post-hybrid (hrs/wk) |
|---|---|---|
| Tech | 6.4 | 2.6 |
| Financial services | 5.8 | 3.1 |
| Healthcare admin | 5.2 | 4.0 |

A labor economist writes that the shift to hybrid schedules reduced weekly commute hours most dramatically for tech workers. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Financial services workers saw their weekly commute fall from 5.8 to 3.1 hours, the second-largest drop in the table.
Healthcare admin workers continued commuting 4.0 hours per week after the hybrid shift.
Tech workers' commutes fell by 3.8 hours per week (from 6.4 to 2.6), compared with 2.7 for financial services (5.8 to 3.1) and 1.2 for healthcare admin (5.2 to 4.0).
All three company types saw their weekly commute hours decrease after adopting hybrid schedules.

explanation: The claim singles out tech as the 'most dramatic' reduction, which requires comparing the size of each drop. Only choice C computes all three differences (3.8, 2.7, 1.2) and shows tech's is largest.

stem:
A team of ornithologists tracked the percentage of nesting songbirds that successfully fledged chicks in three habitat types across a five-year restoration project. The table summarizes fledging rates at the project's start and end.

| Habitat | 2019 fledge rate | 2024 fledge rate |
|---|---|---|
| Restored wetland | 38% | 67% |
| Old-growth forest | 55% | 59% |
| Suburban edge | 22% | 41% |

The lead ornithologist argues that the project's wetland restoration had a greater effect on songbird fledging success than any other habitat change in the study. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this argument?

choices.text: The restored wetland had a 2024 fledge rate of 67%, the highest in the table.
Old-growth forest continued to have a fledge rate above 55% throughout the study.
Suburban edge nests nearly doubled in success, rising from 22% to 41%.
Fledging in the restored wetland climbed from 38% to 67% — a 29-point jump, compared with a 19-point rise at the suburban edge and only a 4-point change in old-growth forest.

explanation: To show the wetland had a 'greater effect' than other habitats, the change in each habitat must be compared. Only choice D calculates all three differences and identifies wetland's 29-point rise as the largest.

stem:
A public health researcher examined youth physical activity at four schools before and after each school added a morning movement break. The table shows the percentage of students meeting the daily 60-minute activity guideline.

| School | Before break | After break |
|---|---|---|
| Lincoln | 29% | 48% |
| Jefferson | 34% | 52% |
| Madison | 31% | 45% |
| Monroe | 27% | 47% |

The researcher claims that adding a morning movement break raised the share of students meeting the 60-minute guideline by at least 14 percentage points at every school studied. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Jefferson's share climbed from 34% to 52%, the highest 'after' rate in the table.
Monroe went from 27% before to 47% after the break was introduced.
Every school showed gains of at least 14 points: Lincoln +19, Jefferson +18, Madison +14, and Monroe +20.
All four schools saw increases in the percentage of students meeting the activity guideline.

explanation: The claim quantifies a minimum gain at every school, so the support must compute each school's change and confirm all exceed 14 points. Only choice C does this, listing +19, +18, +14, and +20.

stem:
A consumer organization tested the battery life of four wireless earbuds under identical conditions. The table reports playback hours per charge and total price.

| Model | Playback (hrs) | Price (USD) |
|---|---|---|
| AirNote | 7.2 | 199 |
| BeatLite | 5.8 | 99 |
| Cadence Pro | 9.4 | 249 |
| Drift X | 6.1 | 129 |

A reviewer writes that BeatLite offers the best playback time per dollar among the tested models. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this statement?

choices.text: Cadence Pro has the longest playback time at 9.4 hours per charge.
BeatLite delivers about 0.059 playback hours per dollar (5.8 ÷ 99), exceeding AirNote's 0.036, Cadence Pro's 0.038, and Drift X's 0.047.
BeatLite is the cheapest model in the table at $99.
All four models offer more than 5 hours of playback per charge.

explanation: A 'best per-dollar' claim requires computing the hours/price ratio for each model and showing BeatLite's is the highest. Only choice B provides those ratios from the table values, confirming 0.059 beats 0.036, 0.038, and 0.047.

stem:
A sociologist surveyed library patrons in three neighborhoods about their monthly visits before and after the branches extended evening hours. The table shows average visits per patron.

| Neighborhood | Pre-extension | Post-extension |
|---|---|---|
| Oakridge | 2.4 | 3.9 |
| Harborview | 1.8 | 4.2 |
| Midtown | 3.1 | 4.0 |

A policy brief cites the survey to argue that extended hours roughly doubled patron visits in at least one neighborhood. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this argument?

choices.text: Oakridge patrons averaged 3.9 visits per month after hours were extended.
Midtown visits rose from 3.1 to 4.0, an increase of about one per patron per month.
At Harborview, average visits climbed from 1.8 to 4.2 per month — about 2.3 times the pre-extension level.
All three branches saw visits rise after the extended hours took effect.

explanation: The claim specifies 'roughly doubled' in at least one branch, so the support needs a ratio near 2x. Only choice C shows Harborview's 4.2/1.8 ≈ 2.3x increase.

stem:
A coastal county reported annual hurricane preparedness kit distribution at three community centers over two years. The table shows kits distributed.

| Center | 2023 kits | 2024 kits |
|---|---|---|
| Cypress | 640 | 880 |
| Palm Grove | 520 | 910 |
| Shoreline | 700 | 770 |

A county emergency manager claims that Palm Grove saw the largest percentage increase in kit distribution from 2023 to 2024. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Palm Grove handed out 910 kits in 2024, the highest single-year total in the table.
Palm Grove's distribution rose from 520 kits in 2023 to 910 in 2024, a 75% increase, compared with Cypress's 38% rise (640 → 880) and Shoreline's 10% rise (700 → 770).
Shoreline distributed 770 kits in 2024, up from 700 the previous year.
All three centers distributed more hurricane preparedness kits in 2024 than in 2023.

explanation: A 'largest percentage increase' claim needs each center's percent change computed. Only choice B calculates 75%, 38%, and 10% to show Palm Grove's jump is the largest.

stem:
A district's biology department tested three variations of a lab activity to compare student post-lab quiz scores. The table shows the average quiz score (out of 20) and the percentage of students earning at least 16/20.

| Lab version | Avg. score | % scoring ≥ 16 |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional | 12.4 | 28% |
| Guided inquiry | 15.1 | 54% |
| Full inquiry | 16.2 | 62% |

The department chair argues that moving from the traditional lab to a full-inquiry version delivered gains on both the average score and the percentage of high scorers. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this argument?

choices.text: The full-inquiry version produced a 16.2 average score, the highest in the table.
Guided inquiry already improved average scores from 12.4 to 15.1.
Switching from traditional to full inquiry raised the class average from 12.4 to 16.2 and lifted the share of students scoring ≥ 16 from 28% to 62%.
More than half of students in both inquiry versions scored at least 16 out of 20.

explanation: The argument specifies gains on two distinct metrics, so the support must cite both. Only choice C pairs the change in average score (12.4 → 16.2) with the change in high scorers (28% → 62%).

stem:
A regional airline tracked on-time performance and passenger complaints per 1,000 passengers at four hubs during one quarter. The table reports both figures.

| Hub | On-time % | Complaints per 1,000 |
|---|---|---|
| Ashford | 72% | 4.1 |
| Bellmark | 88% | 1.2 |
| Crestwood | 81% | 2.6 |
| Deerfield | 66% | 5.3 |

An airline analyst claims that, across these hubs, higher on-time performance is consistently paired with fewer complaints per 1,000 passengers. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this claim?

choices.text: Bellmark had both the highest on-time rate at 88% and the lowest complaint rate at 1.2 per 1,000, while Deerfield had the lowest on-time rate at 66% and the highest complaint rate at 5.3.
Crestwood was on time 81% of the time, with 2.6 complaints per 1,000 passengers.
Ashford's complaint rate of 4.1 per 1,000 was higher than Bellmark's or Crestwood's.
All four hubs had on-time performance rates between 66% and 88%.

explanation: A 'consistent pairing' claim is best supported by showing the extremes of both metrics align at the same hubs. Only choice A contrasts Bellmark (best on both) with Deerfield (worst on both), directly demonstrating the inverse relationship.

stem:
A researcher studying urban heat measured July average rooftop temperatures at four city blocks before and after each block added reflective ('cool') roofing. The table shows the temperatures in degrees Celsius.

| Block | Pre-cool roof (°C) | Post-cool roof (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Ash | 48.2 | 39.4 |
| Birch | 45.6 | 38.0 |
| Cedar | 50.1 | 41.7 |
| Dogwood | 46.8 | 40.2 |

The researcher concludes that installing reflective roofing produced an average rooftop temperature drop of more than 7°C across the studied blocks. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this conclusion?

choices.text: Cedar saw the largest drop, from 50.1°C to 41.7°C.
Every block's post-cool-roof temperature is below 42°C.
The individual drops were 8.8°C (Ash), 7.6°C (Birch), 8.4°C (Cedar), and 6.6°C (Dogwood), averaging about 7.9°C — more than the 7°C threshold.
Dogwood's rooftop temperature fell from 46.8°C to 40.2°C after the install.

explanation: The conclusion specifies an average drop above 7°C, which requires computing all four differences and averaging them. Only choice C actually performs that calculation, yielding ~7.9°C to clear the 7°C threshold.

stem:
A transit planner assessed four bus routes for potential upgrades by recording average daily riders, on-time arrival rate, and cost per rider. The table shows the values.

| Route | Daily riders | On-time % | Cost per rider |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 2,400 | 88% | $1.20 |
| R2 | 3,100 | 74% | $1.55 |
| R3 | 1,800 | 92% | $2.10 |
| R4 | 2,700 | 81% | $1.35 |

The planner argues that Route R2 is the best candidate for upgrades because it carries the most riders yet has the weakest on-time record of any route studied. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this argument?

choices.text: R3 has the highest on-time rate at 92%, making it a strong performer.
R2 carries 3,100 daily riders, the highest total in the table, and has an on-time rate of 74%, the lowest figure shown.
R4's 2,700 daily riders and $1.35 cost per rider make it an efficient route.
R1 has the lowest cost per rider at $1.20 and an 88% on-time rate.

explanation: The argument hinges on R2 having simultaneously the most riders and the worst on-time rate, so the support must establish both extremes for that route. Only choice B names R2's top ridership (3,100) and bottom on-time figure (74%) together.

stem:
An agricultural scientist compared the water efficiency of three irrigation techniques on maize plots, tracking water used (liters per kg of grain) and yield (kg per hectare). The table summarizes one growing season.

| Technique | Water (L/kg grain) | Yield (kg/ha) |
|---|---|---|
| Flood | 3,100 | 6,200 |
| Sprinkler | 2,400 | 6,500 |
| Drip | 1,600 | 7,100 |

The scientist concludes that, compared with flood irrigation, drip irrigation simultaneously cut water use by nearly half and raised maize yields per hectare. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support this conclusion?

choices.text: Sprinkler irrigation used 2,400 liters per kilogram of grain and produced 6,500 kg/ha.
Drip irrigation used 1,600 liters of water per kilogram of grain, less than flood's 3,100 (a 48% reduction) while increasing yield from 6,200 to 7,100 kg/ha (+900 kg/ha).
Flood irrigation required the most water per kilogram of grain in the table.
Drip irrigation produced the highest yield at 7,100 kg/ha.

explanation: The conclusion makes two simultaneous claims (near-halved water use AND higher yield) that must both be quantified against flood irrigation. Only choice B provides both the 48% water cut and the 900 kg/ha yield gain.
